name: Musa and the Children of Israel, part 2
epithet: A nation handed everything, still learning to obey

era.period: Musa and the Children of Israel
era.place: After the sea, in the wilderness

title: Musa and the Children of Israel, part 2: the cow, Qarun, and the knowledge of al-Khidr
meta_description: Day 23 of 29: three scenes from Musa's people, the cow they argued into a mountain, Qarun's wealth swallowed by the earth, and Musa humbled by al-Khidr. A nation given everything, still learning the one thing: obey. Retold faithfully from Mufti Ismail Menk's Stories of the Prophets.
keywords: Musa and Bani Israil, the cow of Bani Israel, al-Baqarah, Qarun, Qarun swallowed, Musa and al-Khidr, Surah al-Kahf, Mufti Menk, stories of the prophets, obedience, Buruja

video.title: Stories Of The Prophets-23~Musa [Moses] and Bani Israel - Part 2

source_note: Retold faithfully from Mufti Ismail Menk's Stories of the Prophets, episode 23 (Musa and the Children of Israel, part 2). Qur'an: Sahih International, verified via quran.ai. The narration is Mufti Menk's, the phrasing is Buruja's.
lede: By now you have watched Musa, peace be upon him, carried as a baby into the house that hunted him, raised, called, sent back to Pharaoh, and walked through a sea that closed over his enemy. You would think a people saved like that would never hesitate again. This is the day that quietly tells you otherwise.

Mufti Menk does not march us through every remaining year of the wandering. He stops at three scenes from the life of Musa that the Qur'an froze in place: a cow they could have slaughtered in a minute and instead argued into a mountain, a rich man named Qarun the earth swallowed whole, and the day Allah humbled the man who spoke to Him by sending him to learn from someone else. Three scenes, one lesson running underneath all of them: when the instruction comes, obey it.

sections.h2: One believer in the house of a tyrant

ayah.arabic: وَضَرَبَ ٱللَّهُ مَثَلًا لِّلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱمْرَأَتَ فِرْعَوْنَ إِذْ قَالَتْ رَبِّ ٱبْنِ لِى عِندَكَ بَيْتًا فِى ٱلْجَنَّةِ وَنَجِّنِى مِن فِرْعَوْنَ وَعَمَلِهِۦ وَنَجِّنِى مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّٰلِمِينَ
ayah.text: And Allāh presents an example of those who believed: the wife of Pharaoh, when she said, "My Lord, build for me near You a house in Paradise and save me from Pharaoh and his deeds and save me from the wrongdoing people."

paras: Before the three scenes, Mufti Menk pauses on a single woman, because she is the answer to the excuse most of us live by. She was the wife of Pharaoh himself, the woman who had raised the infant Musa in the palace, and she knew. She knew the boy spoke the truth and her husband spoke a lie, and she stood inside the very engine room of the tyranny and refused to let it own her heart. Her du'a is preserved in the Qur'an forever.

Listen to the order of her words, Mufti Menk says, because she teaches you how to ask. She did not say, build for me a house in Paradise near You. She said, build for me near You a house, and only then, in Paradise. Nearness to Allah came first; the house came after. Once you are close to Him you already have everything. And notice where she was when she said it: surrounded, outnumbered, every face around her in the wrong. It is the position of the convert whose whole family is elsewhere, of the one person in a room who knows the joke is cruel and will not laugh. To stand out like that is hard. But Allah mentions His good servants by name to the angels, and a soul who refuses to drown in the crowd is exactly the kind of name He loves to mention.

sections.h2: The cow they argued into a mountain

ayah.arabic: وَإِذْ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ لِقَوْمِهِۦٓ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَأْمُرُكُمْ أَن تَذْبَحُوا۟ بَقَرَةً ۖ قَالُوٓا۟ أَتَتَّخِذُنَا هُزُوًا ۖ قَالَ أَعُوذُ بِٱللَّهِ أَنْ أَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْجَٰهِلِينَ
ayah.text: And [recall] when Moses said to his people, "Indeed, Allāh commands you to slaughter a cow." They said, "Do you take us in ridicule?" He said, "I seek refuge in Allāh from being among the ignorant."

paras: A wealthy man had been murdered, and the killing was tearing the community apart: family against family, everyone accusing, no one confessing. They brought the problem to Musa, and Allah gave him an answer so simple it sounds like it cannot be the answer. Slaughter a cow. Any cow. Do it, and the matter will be settled. The whole thing could have ended that afternoon.

Instead they began to ask. What kind of cow? So Allah told them: not too old, not too young, somewhere in between. They could have slaughtered one then. Instead: what colour? A rich, radiant yellow, the Qur'an answered, pleasing to the eye of anyone who looks at it. Still they pressed: the cows all look alike to us, describe it exactly. And so the description tightened around them like a net of their own weaving: a cow never worked to plough or to water the fields, flawless, without a single mark. Here is the heart of it, Mufti Menk says, drawn straight from the words shaddadu fa shaddada Allah alayhim: they made it difficult, so Allah made it difficult for them. Obedience would have cost them one ordinary cow. Their delay cost them a hunt across the land for the one impossible animal that now existed only because their questions had conjured it.

sections.h2: Now you have come with the truth

ayah.arabic: قَالَ إِنَّهُۥ يَقُولُ إِنَّهَا بَقَرَةٌ لَّا ذَلُولٌ تُثِيرُ ٱلْأَرْضَ وَلَا تَسْقِى ٱلْحَرْثَ مُسَلَّمَةٌ لَّا شِيَةَ فِيهَا ۚ قَالُوا۟ ٱلْـَٰٔنَ جِئْتَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۚ فَذَبَحُوهَا وَمَا كَادُوا۟ يَفْعَلُونَ
ayah.text: He said, "He says, 'It is a cow neither trained to plow the earth nor to irrigate the field, one free from fault with no spot upon her.'" They said, "Now you have come with the truth." So they slaughtered her, but they could hardly do it.

paras: The cow they were now forced to find belonged, it is reported, to an orphan and his widowed mother. The boy's dying father had owned only a single calf, and he had released it into the forest and left his wife and child in Allah's care, trusting Him over a society he knew would seize a weak family's wealth. Allah guarded that calf in the wild until the day the people, desperate, came looking for exactly it. The price they paid for the cow's hide filled with gold made the orphan rich in an afternoon. Mufti Menk lingers here, because the same Allah who let them complicate their own lives was quietly running a rescue for a fatherless boy whose father had simply trusted Him.

And then the purpose. They struck the dead man with a piece of the slaughtered cow, and by the leave of Allah he sat up alive, named his killer, and lay back down. Thus does Allah bring the dead to life, the verse says, and He shows you His signs that you might reason. Three lessons fall out of one strange story, Mufti Menk counts them off: the raw power of Allah over life and death, the justice He insists upon, and the one that is meant for us, that when the truth arrives we surrender to it instead of arguing it into a mountain. This is why Allah named an entire surah, the longest in the Qur'an, after this cow: al-Baqarah. The whole chapter your eyes pass every time you open the Qur'an carries the title of a people who could not just obey.

sections.h2: Qarun, and the wealth the earth swallowed

ayah.arabic: إِنَّ قَٰرُونَ كَانَ مِن قَوْمِ مُوسَىٰ فَبَغَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ ۖ وَءَاتَيْنَٰهُ مِنَ ٱلْكُنُوزِ مَآ إِنَّ مَفَاتِحَهُۥ لَتَنُوٓأُ بِٱلْعُصْبَةِ أُو۟لِى ٱلْقُوَّةِ إِذْ قَالَ لَهُۥ قَوْمُهُۥ لَا تَفْرَحْ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْفَرِحِينَ
ayah.text: Indeed, Qārūn was from the people of Moses, but he tyrannized them. And We gave him of treasures whose keys would burden a band of strong men; thereupon his people said to him, "Do not exult. Indeed, Allāh does not like the exultant.

paras: The second scene is a man from Musa's own people, Qarun, given wealth so vast that the keys to his treasure stores alone would have buckled the knees of a band of strong men. Imagine, Mufti Menk says, one credit card can keep you up at night; this man's keys were a burden in themselves. And he had one disease. Arrogance. Everything he owned, he insisted, came from himself: my brain, my plan, my cleverness, my effort. I, I, I, and me, me, me. Nothing he had was ever traced back to the One who gave it. When his people warned him, do not exult, seek the home of the Hereafter with what Allah has given you and do not forget your share of this world, do good as Allah has done good to you, he waved them off: I was only given it because of knowledge I have.

Some looked at his gold and ached for it, and Allah preserves their wish word for word: would that we had what Qarun was given. But the people of knowledge answered them, and it is an answer worth memorising: the reward of Allah is better for the one who believes and does righteousness, and none are granted it except the patient. So Qarun went out before his people in his full glittering display, and the earth opened. It swallowed him, his house, and everything he owned, and there was no faction on earth to help him against Allah, nor could he save himself. By morning the very ones who had envied him were saying: it is Allah who extends provision to whom He wills and restricts it; had He not been gracious to us, He would have caused the earth to swallow us too. Mufti Menk draws the line straight to us: never be a show-off, and never let your eyes climb after what Allah gave someone else, because the One who gives has every power to take, and the safest posture before Him is humble, all the way to the ground.

sections.h2: The day Musa was sent to learn

ayah.arabic: فَوَجَدَا عَبْدًا مِّنْ عِبَادِنَآ ءَاتَيْنَٰهُ رَحْمَةً مِّنْ عِندِنَا وَعَلَّمْنَٰهُ مِن لَّدُنَّا عِلْمًا
ayah.text: And they found a servant from among Our servants [i.e., al-Khiḍr] to whom We had given mercy from Us and had taught him from Us a [certain] knowledge.

paras: The third scene begins with a question put to Musa: who is the most knowledgeable person alive? He answered, naturally, that it was him, and why not, he received revelation, he spoke to Allah directly. So Allah corrected him gently. No one holds all knowledge; Allah gives it out in pockets, this one excelling here, that one excelling there. There is a servant of Mine, He told Musa, given a knowledge you were not given. And Musa, peace be upon him, the prophet who spoke with Allah, set out on a journey to sit at the feet of someone who could teach him something he did not know. Before the story even moves, Mufti Menk catches the lesson: we are meant to benefit from one another's knowledge, and the people we should keep closest are not the ones who polish this world for us but the ones who can carry us toward the next, the scholars who are the heart's physicians when we are dying of a spiritual illness we cannot even feel.

They found him where the two seas meet, the place a fish had slipped from their basket into the water as a sign. This pious servant, known in the reports as al-Khidr, was not a prophet, Mufti Menk is careful to say, but a righteous man following the law of Musa, given a particular knowledge directly from Allah. Musa asked, with beautiful humility, may I follow you so you can teach me. And al-Khidr warned him: you will never manage to be patient with me, for how can you be patient over things you have not encompassed in knowledge? Musa promised he would try.

sections.h2: How can you be patient over what you do not know?

ayah.arabic: قَالَ هَٰذَا فِرَاقُ بَيْنِى وَبَيْنِكَ ۚ سَأُنَبِّئُكَ بِتَأْوِيلِ مَا لَمْ تَسْتَطِع عَّلَيْهِ صَبْرًا
ayah.text: [Al-Khiḍr] said, "This is parting between me and you. I will inform you of the interpretation of that about which you could not have patience.

paras: Three times Musa could not hold his peace. Al-Khidr bored a hole in a boat that had carried them for free, and Musa cried out, will you drown its people? He killed a young boy they passed, and Musa was aghast: a pure soul, for nothing? He rebuilt a leaning wall for a town that had just refused them so much as a meal, and Musa said, you could at least have taken a wage for it. Each time al-Khidr reminded him: did I not tell you you would never be able to bear with me? At the third, they parted.

And then the meanings, every one of them mercy wearing the mask of harm. The boat belonged to poor men who lived by it, and a king downstream was seizing every sound vessel by force; a damaged boat he would pass over, so the flaw was its rescue. The boy's parents were believers, and Allah knew the child would have grown to crush them with rebellion and disbelief, so He took him and would grant them a better child, purer and nearer to mercy. The wall stood over a buried treasure belonging to two orphans whose father had been a righteous man, and had it collapsed the treasure would have been looted; held up, it would wait until the boys grew to claim what was theirs. Mufti Menk draws out the staggering point underneath: Allah knows not only what was and is and will be, but what was never going to be, and how it would have unfolded had He let it. The boat was never actually going to be seized, because Allah already knew there would be a hole in it. That is the depth of His knowledge, and the smallness of ours: a single drop, against a whole ocean. Musa, peace be upon him, was sent on this journey so the man who spoke to Allah would feel, in his own chest, how little any of us can see.

sections.h2: The obedience these stories were really about
paras: Step back and the three scenes rhyme. A people who could have obeyed in a sentence and chose to debate. A man drowned in his own wealth because he never bowed. A prophet learning that the wisdom behind Allah's commands is often hidden from the very people asked to follow them. The thread Mufti Menk keeps pulling is obedience: when the instruction comes, do it, do not stall it into something heavy, do not demand to understand every reason before you submit, because the One issuing the command sees the whole ocean while you are holding a single drop.

And he turns it, near the end, onto our worship, which is exactly where your own faith stands in this story. Look at the pillars, he says. In salah, fasting, and Hajj, we gladly do the obligation and then add far more on top, the sunnah and the voluntary outweighing the fard many times over. But in charity we cling to the bare two and a half percent and grow strangely stingy with anything beyond it. Bani Israil were a nation handed everything, a prophet who spoke to Allah, a sea split open, food sent from the sky, and still the hardest thing they were ever asked to do was simply to obey without arguing. That is the warning folded into this whole pillar: this ummah was told to learn from their example, not to repeat it. So when the command is clear, whether it is the prayer that calls you five times a day or the wealth Allah asks you to loosen your grip on, the believer's answer is the one Bani Israil reached only at the very end, and far too late: now you have come with the truth. We hear, and we obey.

dua.arabic: رَبِّ ٱبْنِ لِى عِندَكَ بَيْتًا فِى ٱلْجَنَّةِ
dua.en: My Lord, build for me near You a house in Paradise. (the du'a of the wife of Pharaoh, Surah at-Tahrim 66:11)

lessons_intro: Three scenes from Musa's people, and one lesson Mufti Menk keeps returning to: the heart of faith is obedience, especially when the wisdom behind the command is hidden from you.

lessons.text: Bani Israil could have slaughtered any cow in a minute. They argued it into a near-impossible one. They made it difficult, so Allah made it difficult for them. When the instruction is clear, the believer's move is to do it, not to stall it.

lessons.bold: Trace every blessing back to the Giver.
lessons.text: Qarun said his wealth came from his own brilliance, and the earth swallowed him whole. Whatever Allah hands you, He can take, and the safest posture before Him is gratitude and humility, not display.

lessons.bold: Do not let your eyes climb after what He gave someone else.
lessons.text: Those who envied Qarun's gold woke to thank Allah they had been spared his fate. The reward of Allah is better for the one who believes and does good, and none are granted it except the patient.

lessons.bold: Your knowledge is a drop; His is the ocean.
lessons.text: Even Musa, who spoke to Allah, was sent to learn from al-Khidr that the wisdom behind events is usually hidden. The harm you resent may be a mercy you cannot yet see, so trust the One who commands.

lessons.bold: Stand out for the truth, even alone.
lessons.text: The wife of Pharaoh believed inside the house of the tyrant himself. One sincere soul who refuses to drown in the crowd is a name Allah mentions to His angels.

faq.q: Where in the Qur'an are these three stories?
faq.a: The cow of Bani Israil is in Surah al-Baqarah 2:67-73, the chapter named after it (al-Baqarah means 'the cow'). Qarun and the earth swallowing his wealth is in Surah al-Qasas 28:76-82. Musa's journey with al-Khidr is in Surah al-Kahf 18:60-82. Mufti Menk also opens with the du'a of the wife of Pharaoh in Surah at-Tahrim 66:11.

Mufti Menk is careful here: al-Khidr was not a prophet but a righteous servant of Allah who followed the law of Musa, peace be upon him, and was given a particular knowledge directly from Allah (Surah al-Kahf 18:65). His name is given in the reports, not in the Qur'an itself, which calls him 'a servant from among Our servants'. The lesson is that Allah distributes knowledge in pockets, and even the one who spoke to Allah was sent to learn what he had not been given.

faq.q: Why is the longest chapter of the Qur'an named after a cow?
faq.a: Surah al-Baqarah ('the Cow') takes its name from this very incident: a people who could have obeyed Allah's command to slaughter a cow with a single ordinary animal, and instead argued and delayed until the requirement narrowed to one near-impossible cow. Naming the longest surah after it keeps the lesson in front of every reader: when the truth and the command arrive, surrender to them rather than complicating them.

faq.q: How does this connect to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and to my own worship?
faq.a: This ummah, the followers of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, were given the stories of Bani Israil to learn from their example, not to repeat it. Their hardest test was simply obeying without arguing. Mufti Menk turns this onto our worship: when the command is clear, whether it is the prayer Allah asks of us or the charity He asks us to give beyond the obligation, the believer's answer is 'we hear and we obey', the answer Bani Israil reached only too late.

faq.q: What does the al-Khidr story teach about hardship I do not understand?
faq.a: Every act of al-Khidr that looked like harm, a damaged boat, a slain boy, a wall rebuilt for free, was in fact a mercy whose reason was hidden until explained. Allah knows what was, what is, what will be, and even what was never going to be and how it would have unfolded. So when you face a loss you cannot make sense of, the story trains you to trust that the One who commands sees the whole ocean while you hold a single drop. Mufti Menk applies it tenderly to a parent who loses a child young: do not drown in the negative, for Allah's wisdom may be far beyond what you can see.

closing_heading: Why this day stays with you
closing: This is the far edge of the Musa story, and it does not close with a miracle or a throne. It closes with a question put to every one of us: when Allah commands, will you obey, or will you argue it into a mountain like the people of the cow, bow to your own wealth like Qarun, or demand to understand every reason before you submit, the way even Musa could not quite hold his patience with al-Khidr? A nation was handed more proof than any people in history, and still the simplest thing, plain obedience, was the thing they fumbled. We were told their story so we would not.

So let it land where your own faith stands, in the prayer you are about to pray and the giving you keep putting off. O Allah, make us of those who hear Your command and answer at once, who trace every blessing back to You and never boast of it, who trust Your wisdom in what we cannot see, and who stand for Your truth even when we stand alone. Build for each of us, near You, a house in Paradise. Ameen.

related.label: Why we pray
related.note: A nation given everything, who still had to learn to obey

slides:
Day 23 of 29.

Musa's people were saved through a split sea. You would think they would never hesitate again.

This is the day that tells you otherwise.

A man was murdered. The community was tearing itself apart.

Allah's answer: slaughter a cow. Any cow. The matter ends today.

Instead, they started asking questions.

What kind of cow? What colour? Describe it exactly.

With every question the description tightened, until only one impossible cow on earth would do.

They made it difficult, so Allah made it difficult for them.

This is why the longest surah in the Qur'an is named al-Baqarah: the Cow.

A whole chapter titled after a people who could not just obey.

Qarun's wealth was so vast the keys alone would buckle strong men.

His disease? 'It came from my own brilliance. Me. I. Mine.'

The earth opened and swallowed him, his house, and all of it.

Those who had envied his gold woke the next morning and said:

had Allah not been gracious to us, the earth would have swallowed us too.

Never let your eyes climb after what Allah gave someone else.

Musa was asked: who is the most knowledgeable alive? He said, me.

So Allah sent him to learn from al-Khidr.

A torn boat, a slain boy, a wall rebuilt for free. Each one a hidden mercy.

Your knowledge is a single drop. His is the whole ocean.

Bani Israil were told to teach us one thing: when the command comes, obey.

We hear, and we obey. #StoriesOfTheProphets
social.caption: Day 23 of 29: Musa and the Children of Israel, part 2. Three scenes the Qur'an froze in place, the cow they argued into a mountain (al-Baqarah 2:67-73), Qarun's wealth swallowed by the earth (al-Qasas 28:76-82), and the day Allah humbled Musa by sending him to learn from al-Khidr (al-Kahf 18:60-82). One lesson underneath all three: when Allah commands, obey, instead of arguing it into something heavy. A nation was handed more proof than any people in history, and still the hardest thing was simply to obey, the same test that meets us in every prayer and every act of giving. Retold faithfully from Mufti Ismail Menk's beloved series (his full lecture is embedded on the site), Qur'an verified via quran.ai. A prophet a day, connected back to the Qur'an and the life of the Prophet ﷺ, at buruja.com. #StoriesOfTheProphets #MuftiMenk #Musa #Quran #alBaqarah #alKahf #Buruja #islam

email.subject: The cow they argued into a mountain
email.alt_subject: A nation given everything, still learning to obey
email.preview: Day 23 of 29: three scenes from Musa's people, and the one thing they could not do.
email.body:
Assalamu alaykum,

By now in the Stories of the Prophets you have watched Musa, peace be upon him, carried into the palace that hunted him, sent back to Pharaoh, and walked through a sea that closed over his enemy. You would think a people saved like that would never hesitate again.

Today is the day that quietly tells you otherwise. Mufti Menk stops at three scenes the Qur'an froze in place.

First, the cow. A man had been murdered and the community was tearing apart, and Allah's answer was simple: slaughter a cow, any cow, and the matter ends. Instead they began to ask. What kind? What colour? Describe it exactly. With every question the description tightened, until only one near-impossible cow on earth would do. They made it difficult, so Allah made it difficult for them (Surah al-Baqarah 2:67-73). This is the very incident the longest chapter of the Qur'an is named after: al-Baqarah, the Cow.

Second, Qarun, a man from Musa's own people given wealth so vast the keys alone would buckle strong men. His disease was arrogance: it all came from my own brilliance, he said. The earth opened and swallowed him whole (Surah al-Qasas 28:76-82), and those who had envied his gold woke to thank Allah they had been spared his fate.

Third, the day Allah humbled the man who spoke to Him. Musa was asked who was the most knowledgeable alive and answered, me, so Allah sent him to learn from al-Khidr (Surah al-Kahf 18:60-82). A torn boat, a slain boy, a wall rebuilt for free, each one a hidden mercy Musa could not yet see. Your knowledge is a single drop; His is the whole ocean.

One lesson runs under all three: when Allah commands, obey, instead of arguing it into something heavy. Bani Israil were handed more proof than any people in history, and still the simplest thing, plain obedience, was the thing they fumbled. This ummah was told their story to learn from it, not to repeat it, which is exactly where it meets your own worship: the prayer you are about to pray, the giving you keep putting off.

Tonight, one du'a, the one a believing woman made inside the house of Pharaoh himself: My Lord, build for me near You a house in Paradise.

Read day 23, and watch Mufti Menk's full lecture, at the link below.
